html:
Sarnafil PVC Systems Under the Sika Name

Sarnafil was a Swiss pioneer in PVC single-ply roofing membrane, and the product line now operates under Sika, the larger Swiss chemical and building-materials company that owns it. On Laredo jobs where PVC's chemical resistance is the deciding factor - buildings near diesel exposure, grease exhaust, or industrial process venting - Sarnafil is one of the manufacturers whose product we specify.

A Swiss PVC Pioneer

Sarnafil's roofing membrane business developed one of the longer manufacturing track records in PVC single-ply roofing specifically, predating a lot of the current single-ply market that later shifted heavily toward TPO. Sika, a large diversified chemical and construction-materials manufacturer, owns and continues the Sarnafil membrane line as part of its broader building-materials portfolio worldwide.

Sika's Broader Construction Chemicals Business

Sika's business extends well beyond roofing membrane into concrete admixtures, waterproofing, sealants, and other construction chemical products used across a wide range of building types. The Sarnafil roofing line is one division within that larger structure rather than a standalone company, which means Sarnafil benefits from Sika's overall corporate scale and research resources even though the roofing product itself is what shows up on the building.

The Membrane Line Itself

Sika Sarnafil's current PVC catalog includes membranes in the standard thickness ranges used across the industry, along with accessory products, fastening systems, and detailing components meant to be specified as a complete assembly. Their reinforced PVC sheets are hot-air welded the same way other manufacturers' PVC products are, and the field welding discipline described on our PVC roof-systems page applies equally to Sarnafil membrane.

Why Track Record Matters on a Multi-Decade Decision

A roof membrane is a decision an owner lives with for decades, and PVC specifically has a somewhat complicated reputation because older-generation PVC products from various manufacturers had plasticizer migration issues that made membranes brittle over time. A manufacturer with Sarnafil's specific length of manufacturing history in PVC gives an owner more field data to look at when asking how a particular formulation ages, compared to a newer entrant into the PVC category. We point owners to that manufacturing history as a factual data point, not a guarantee of performance.

  • PVC formulation and thickness matched to the building's specific chemical exposure risk
  • Field weld schedule planned around morning deck temperatures
  • Every seam probed and results logged by roof zone before project close-out
  • Flashing detail reviewed specifically at exhaust and process-vent penetrations
  • Warranty documentation reviewed with the owner before contract signing

Warranty Structure on Sarnafil Systems

Like every manufacturer we cover, Sika Sarnafil's warranty terms are specific to the product line and installation documentation for a given project. We pull the actual warranty document for the specific membrane being installed rather than describing coverage in general terms, since exclusions and maintenance obligations differ enough between product generations that a generic summary would misrepresent what's actually covered.

Where Sarnafil Fits Among Our Laredo PVC Jobs

Sarnafil competes directly with other PVC manufacturers on the buildings where PVC's chemical resistance is the reason we're specifying single-ply membrane at all - the truck-yard-adjacent warehouses, the restaurant exhaust roofs, the industrial process buildings described on our PVC roof-systems page. We compare Sarnafil's current product data and pricing against those alternatives on a job-by-job basis rather than treating it as an automatic first choice within the PVC category.

Beyond the membrane sheet itself, Sika Sarnafil's catalog includes prefabricated corner and detail pieces meant to speed up the labor-intensive part of PVC installation around curbs, pipe penetrations, and parapet corners. Using manufacturer-fabricated details instead of hand-forming every corner on site reduces the number of field welds a crew has to make in the first place, which matters on a hot Laredo roof where every additional field weld is another opportunity for a heat-related quality issue if the crew is rushing to beat the afternoon temperature.

Sika's broader business extends well beyond roofing membrane into concrete admixtures, waterproofing, and other construction chemical products, and we source accessories and detailing components from the same manufacturer as the membrane wherever possible, since mixing PVC formulations across manufacturers at a field weld can create compatibility questions that are better avoided than argued about later.

Questions About Sika Sarnafil PVC Roofing

Is Sarnafil a different product from other PVC membranes?

It's a distinct manufacturer's formulation with its own long manufacturing history in PVC roofing specifically, though it competes in the same general PVC single-ply category as other manufacturers' products.

Why choose Sarnafil over another PVC manufacturer?

We evaluate Sarnafil against other PVC manufacturers, including Johns Manville's and Sika's own broader catalog, on the same basis as any product decision - fit for the building's specific exposure and cost - rather than defaulting to one brand.

Has Sarnafil PVC had the plasticizer brittleness problems older PVC had?

That's a fair question to raise with the manufacturer directly for the specific product generation being installed on your building; we discuss it honestly rather than making a blanket claim either way.

Is Sika the same company as Sarnafil?

Sika is the parent company that owns and continues the Sarnafil roofing membrane line as part of its broader building-materials business, which also spans concrete admixtures and waterproofing products used well outside of roofing.

What does a Sarnafil warranty typically require for maintenance?

That varies by product line and project documentation; we review the specific warranty document with the owner rather than summarizing generically.
